Transcrição Completa: 33 State Of The Agency Contraction And Expansion

 

[00:00][music]

 

[00:02]In business, sometimes you need to contract to expand.

 

[00:06]There's this great book, it's called The 22 Immutable Laws of Branding, and it saved my business during a time where I was contracting, but I wasn't controlling the contraction.

 

[00:18]There's a few different examples that the book points out. Chapter 3 and Chapter 4. Chapter 3 is the Law of Contraction, Chapter 4 is the Law of Expansion.

 

[00:28]It uses some different examples as a way to showcase which types of businesses that you and I would already be familiar with have contracted and expanded relative to using these laws of branding.

 

[00:40]So, let's use an example like Rolex, okay? You likely know Rolex for the watch, okay?

 

[00:47]You likely don't think of Rolex with t-shirts or rings or necklaces. You likely think Rolex automatically pair it to a watch.

 

[00:55]Because as a luxury brand, they contracted on the amount of services and products that they offered, so they became specialized and well-known in one particular area from contracting what types of services they offered, which then allowed the financial state of their business to exponentially expand.

 

[01:14]As another example of this, the Law of Contraction can go against your best interest, depending on how you use it.

 

[01:21]If you choose to contract who you sell to, that might limit who and how well-known you can become as an agency and as a personal brand.

 

[01:30]If you contract the wrong way, it will break your business. Also, in the same route, if you expand the wrong way, it will break your business as well. It'll make you less known, less valuable.

 

[01:42]Let's use an example of Subway with the Law of Contraction. Okay, Subway is one of the most well-known, I believe still to date it is the most franchised business around the planet.

 

[01:54]That comes from contracting the types of services they sell.

 

[01:58]Think of your local corner store, gas station, maybe your local 7-Eleven, depending on where you're at in the world, you might know what I'm talking about, you might not.

 

[02:07]But, whatever, you got a station near you that sells a bunch of random stuff. Chips, candies, they probably have that like hot dog stand that sells all that other weird stuff on the rollers, too, that I hope people don't buy.

 

[02:21]Maybe they sell pizza there, or I've seen one 7-Eleven that sells like nachos and wings inside of it.

 

[02:27]They sell drinks, they sell alcohol, maybe outside they sell gas, they got all your candies, of course.

 

[02:33]Maybe they even imported some candies from other parts of the world to make it a little more unique to them.

 

[02:38]Maybe they have some general stuff for the house or to fix your vehicle. They probably got oil and all just all kinds of stuff, antifreeze, everything.

 

[02:47]But, those stores have a tough time expanding how known they become. They have a tough time expanding their revenues exponentially because they sell too much stuff.

 

[02:58]Subway, as a key example here, focused on a key service. They said, "We have a footlong sub, and we are going to stay in our lane.

 

[03:08]We are going to offer, just like any other deli that's in your local market that has kind of the similar setup as a gas station. They'll probably have the meats, the cheeses, the veggies. Maybe they'll have their own little deli bar."

 

[03:21]But, they also, just like a gas station or 7-Eleven, sell a bunch of other random stuff. Subway doesn't do that.

 

[03:28]They sell you chips and a drink. That is their upsell. And depending on the location, maybe they have soups and salads, too.

 

[03:36]They have a 12-inch sub, or they have a 6-inch sub. You know, you really can't get many more services or products there. I think you can buy some cookies.

 

[03:46]Everything that Subway sells is a complimentary service to its main product that it has become known for.

 

[03:53]So, in your agency, let's say you're selling Facebook advertising. Let's say that you're selling chatbots, or whatever it is that you choose to sell as a service.

 

[04:03]You're a video guy, graphic designer, whatever, right? You choose to sell that one service, and you become known for that service, you become a specialist in that service.

 

[04:13]And come time when you say to yourself, "You know, I could probably start selling some other products right now. I could probably start selling some other services. What should I do?"

 

[04:23]Don't make the mistake that I did, where because I had more staff with more skills, I chose to sell more of these other services. They didn't necessarily complement my main service.

 

[04:35]All of the sudden, we were advertisers who were also selling funnels, and we were selling email marketing, and we were selling creating info products for people, and editing together the whole video product through and through everything.

 

[04:49]Although we were servicing one industry, one niche, or niche, however you say it, that did not enable me to expand my financial means.

 

[04:59]It actually contracted the amount of money that I was making every month because I had more expenses, I took on more clients, and I became less known in one particular area where we specialized more than anywhere else.

 

[05:13]At a future point in my business, when I picked up that 22 Immutable Laws of Branding, changed my entire perspective.

 

[05:21]The Law of Expansion is the law that really took my thinking to the next level and helped save my business when my financial situation in my business was going down instead of up, while I was selling more services thinking that it should go up.

 

[05:36]The Law of Expansion says, let's think of American Express, okay?

 

[05:41]At one point in the past, American Express, they owned 27% of the market share for the entire credit card industry. Today they own 14%.

 

[05:52]A few years ago, they had a new CEO named Tom come in and Tom decided every year they would come out with 13 new types of credit cards to offer new types of consumers an American Express card.

 

[06:07]So they said, "Rather than just offering the prestigious market credit cards, we are going to try to get new demographics of lower financially tiered people to have an American Express and have that opportunity."

 

[06:20]What Tom did backfired the amount of market share that they had.

 

[06:25]So he thought in his head expanding the products and services that they offered towards additional demographics on top of it. He thought that that would expand their revenues, but it did the opposite.

 

[06:37]He expanded the types of products that he had, who he was selling to, and all of a sudden American Express had over a 10% drop in the amount of market share they had, which is substantial in the financial market, especially at the level they were operating at.

 

[06:53]Mercedes-Benz is another key example. As a player in the automotive space, they've always been prestigious and a luxury brand.

 

[07:01]They've always kept their automotive products in a particular tier of pricing to keep themselves away from the lower-end models.

 

[07:09]Just as Tom did in American Express, Mercedes had a new executive come in and convinced all the other executives that it would be a good idea to expand their overall market share and to expand the amount of financial revenue that was coming in each month to come out with some new cars that were lower priced and that that would draw in new customers, expand the revenue, just overall bring additional market share.

 

[07:35]Once again, a key example of it doing the opposite.

 

[07:39]Because Mercedes, instead of catering to their existing demographic, the people that they were already selling to, the people that were already giving them and building their business with them, they chose to stop selling additional services to those people, to that demographic.

 

[07:54]They chose to bring in a new demographic with new products. Backfired. They lost rev share, they lost market share, and overall it was a bad decision.

 

[08:04]Now in my agency, as an example just to bring this out of metaphors into context for what you and I would use it for as these two laws, law of contraction, law of expansion.

 

[08:14]I chose when I had a graphic designer, a video guy. I ended up having two of them. I ended up having like different different skills, you know, a multiple of different skills.

 

[08:25]I had a sales team of 11 people at one point. I had some PR connections. I had a bunch of digital marketers inside of my agency.

 

[08:33]And because I had all these positions, I said, "You know what? There are so many different types of services that we could sell. Let's become the agency that sells a bunch of stuff to this one demographic.

 

[08:44]Hey, instead of just taking the world's largest personality brands that do the best financially, let's go ahead and let's take on some lower ones too. We'll take on people and we'll help develop them as they are progressing as a personal brand."

 

[08:58]Poof. Did the quite opposite of what I intended it to do, just as I could have learned the hard way, or the easier way that is, through Mercedes and the American Express example.

 

[09:10]I and my agency, when I expanded the services that I offered and I expanded the demographic I was selling to, contracted my revenue financially.

 

[09:21]So because and when I say this book saved my business, I read this book while my business was contracting, but while I was expanding my services, and it blew my mind.

 

[09:32]I said, "Wow, this is so simple. I've got to stop offering as many services as I am to all these new people."

 

[09:39]I redefined my agency in that moment. We only work with the world's largest personality brands and we are rainmakers.

 

[09:47]We only offer services that are the highest revenue-driven actions for whoever we want to work with, period. That's it.

 

[09:55]All of a sudden, when I made that decision, I fired all those low-ticket clients or just the people that we started selling to that didn't fit that specific criteria, boom, my revenue expanded 4x from what it was at that time where it contracted.

 

[10:10]So long story short, the law of expansion and contraction can really play into your favor or it can really play against you, depending on which side of the law you want to be on.

 

[10:21][laughter]

 

[10:22]I'll see you guys in the next video.

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

American Express: 27% de market share (passado) → 14% de market share (atual)

Queda de mais de 10% de market share para American Express

13 novos tipos de cartões de crédito lançados anualmente pela American Express

Equipe de vendas de 11 pessoas na agência do palestrante

Expansão de receita de 4x após contração estratégica

Capítulos 3 e 4 do livro "The 22 Immutable Laws of Branding"

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

Lei da Contração: Reduzir produtos/serviços para se especializar e se tornar conhecido em uma área específica

Lei da Expansão: Expandir incorretamente (muitos produtos ou novos demograficos) contrai receita

Modelo Rolex: Especialização em relógios de luxo, evitando diversificação para camisetas, anéis, colares

Modelo Subway: Foco em footlong subs com upsells complementares (chips, bebidas, cookies, sopas, saladas)

Estratégia de Reposicionamento: Focar apenas nos maiores personal brands do mundo como "rainmakers"

Serviços Complementares: Vender apenas serviços que complementam o produto principal

Firing de Clientes: Demitir clientes low-ticket que não se encaixam no critério específico

 

ESTRUTURA:

Framework de Contração Correta: Contrair serviços para se especializar, não contrair demografia

Framework de Expansão Incorreta: Expandir serviços + expandir demografia = contração de receita

Estrutura de Posicionamento: "Trabalhar apenas com os maiores personal brands do mundo" + "Oferecer apenas ações de maior geração de receita"

Modelo de Serviços Complementares: Produto principal + upsells que complementam (não produtos aleatórios)

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

Caso American Express: Perda de 13% de market share ao expandir para 13 novos produtos/ano

Caso Mercedes-Benz: Perda de market share e receita ao lançar carros de menor preço

Caso da Agência do Palestrante:

Expansão de serviços (ads + funnels + email marketing + info products + edição de vídeo) = contração de receita

Mais despesas + mais clientes = menos dinheiro por mês

Após contração estratégica: expansão de receita de 4x

Comparação 7-Eleven vs Subway: Lojas que vendem tudo têm dificuldade em expandir receita exponencialmente vs especialização em subs

 

CASOS DE ESTUDO:

Rolex: Contração em relógios de luxo → expansão financeira exponencial

Subway: Contração em footlong subs → franquia mais expandida do planeta

American Express: Expansão de 13 cartões/ano para novos demograficos → perda de 13% market share

Mercedes-Benz: Expansão para carros de menor preço → perda de market share e receita

Agência do Palestrante: Expansão de serviços + demografia → contração de receita → Recontração estratégica → 4x expansão de receita

 

DURAÇÃO: 10:25 FORMATO: Vídeo educacional / Aula de programa de agência de marketing
